nativeRange.habitat: Found along outer reef drop-offs, where it lives secretively among crevices and clefts in caves, typically at depths of 20 to 110 metres.

diet.notes: In the wild the natural diet is undocumented, but captive specimens are generally offered Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, frozen mysis shrimp, and other meaty items. Abundant live rock for microalgae grazing is considered important.

avoidWith: aggressive or large boisterous fish that may outcompete the initially reclusive specimen

summary: Colin's Angelfish is a rare and secretive pygmy angelfish from deep outer reef drop-offs across the Indo-West Pacific. Known scientifically as Centropyge colini, it is infrequently collected and widely considered one of the most demanding dwarf angelfishes to keep, suited only to expert aquarists.
careNotes: Colin's Angelfish (Centropyge colini) is rarely collected for the aquarium trade and is regarded as an expert-only species. The older name Paracentropyge colini still appears in some aquarium trade sources, but Centropyge colini is the currently accepted scientific name. Newly introduced specimens tend to be very shy and reclusive, and are best added to an aquarium before any larger or more boisterous fish are introduced. A generous amount of live rock arranged with caves and crevices is considered essential, both to give the fish a sense of security and to provide microalgae for grazing. It is generally advised to keep only one dwarf angelfish (genus Centropyge) per display, as introducing a second species from this group is likely to cause conflict. In the wild, this species forms harems of three to seven individuals — one dominant male and several females — and is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females can change sex to male when the dominant male is absent. In aquariums, Colin's Angelfish is most often kept as a single specimen or as a proven pair. The species has been reported to occasionally nip at SPS coral fragments (small-polyp stony corals), so it is considered reef-compatible with caution rather than fully reef-safe.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Colin's Angelfish has not been documented in the hobby literature. In the wild, the species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals are born female and the socially dominant fish in a group transitions to male. Harems in the wild consist of a single male accompanied by several females.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9.0 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 3 in / 7.62 cm — averaged to 7–9 cm range), temperature (26.2–28.7 °C vs LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6 °C — averaged to 24–27 °C, flagged needs_review), native range, taxonomy, and harem behavior. FishBase lists the species as Centropyge colini; LiveAquaria uses the older genus placement Paracentropyge colini.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented. In the wild this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems, with the dominant fish in each group being male.
